fields.description:
# S7 — End-to-end proof + backup/restore drill + monitoring + runbooks

## Why
Prove the whole gate works end-to-end and that freeze-and-restore is real. This is the
dogfood-readiness checkpoint for d251 (P1 integrity proven on a real change; P3 restore
proven). The full four-pass dogfood is b744-WS6.

## What
- **E2E proof:** push a change -> the bot reviews + votes `LLM-Review` -> submit
  (whole-topic) -> replicate -> the change appears on GitHub `main`; AND a direct GitHub
  push / PR-merge to `main` is rejected.
  - **`LLM-Review` label-integer vocabulary (so the e2e test can assert a concrete value).**
    The `LLM-Review` label gates CODE merges, so the review-bot casts it from the
    **CODE-review verdict** (`PASS`/`BLOCK`, see `src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/decide.py`)
    of the change under review. The e2e test asserts the integer for a *passing*
    change. The mapping the test must assume: a passing CODE review votes **MAX** (the value
    `label:LLM-Review=MAX` resolves to in d251's `project.config` label range — the e2e
    test reads the achieved label value from Gerrit and asserts it equals that range's MAX,
    rather than hardcoding `+1` vs `+2`), which is what makes the change submit-enabling;
    a blocking CODE review casts a non-MAX value (the label's MIN, e.g. -1, fail-closed) so
    Gerrit refuses submit. The exact integers are pinned by d251's `project.config` label
    definition — this AC cross-references that, so the e2e test and the project config agree
    on MAX/MIN by construction rather than by a guessed literal.
  - **No-vote / blocked fallback (e2e harness robustness).** The e2e test script must
    bound the wait for the `LLM-Review` vote with an explicit timeout and must distinguish
    three terminal states: (a) **MAX vote + submit succeeds** = pass; (b) **non-MAX (MIN)
    vote** (a blocking CODE review, fail-closed) = "gate correctly blocked" (expected only in
    the negative-path sub-test, a failure in the positive path); (c) **no vote within the
    timeout** = "voter/harness failed" — surfaced as a distinct, non-ambiguous harness error,
    NOT silently conflated with a correctly-blocked change. This makes a model
    outage/timeout during the drill diagnosable rather than a hang.
- **Backup/restore — two snapshot tiers (reconciled).** (a) ROUTINE RPO safety net: DLM takes
  scheduled, **crash-consistent** EBS snapshots of the data volume — these are NOT
  application-quiesced, which is acceptable because Gerrit's NoteDb is git (crash-consistent and
  recoverable, `git fsck` on restore). (b) ON-DEMAND QUIESCED snapshot: before a PLANNED restore
  drill / upgrade / migration, briefly stop the Gerrit container (or set it read-only), take the
  snapshot, restart — so that restore source is application-consistent. `index/` + `cache/` are
  rebuildable and excluded from any `$site_path` archive. Run the restore-from-IaC drill (on a
  separate instance, per the safety bullet) and record the achieved RTO; merged code is always
  safe on GitHub (RPO = only in-flight reviews since the last snapshot, bounded by the DLM interval).
  - **RTO target (pre-stated, not circular).** A numeric **RTO target of ≤ 2 hours** is the
    pass/fail bound, agreed *before* the drill and documented in the restore runbook. The
    drill records the *achieved* RTO and passes only if it is within that 2-hour target
    (the target is the baseline; the achieved time is measured against it). 2 h is the
    starting target for this single-instance, IaC-rebuild + `$site_path`-restore topology;
    the runbook may revise it for a later drill, but a drill never sets its own target.
  - **Snapshot retention.** The DLM lifecycle policy carries a bounded retention
    (`retain_rule { count = N }`, default **N = 7**) and the `$site_path` snapshots carry an
    equivalent TTL/count so snapshots do not accumulate unbounded; the restore runbook states
    how to identify the latest valid snapshot for a drill.
  - **Restore-drill safety (non-destructive by construction).** The restore DRILL provisions
    a SEPARATE, throwaway instance + volume RESTORED FROM THE LATEST SNAPSHOT (a scratch
    Terraform workspace / a `-var` toggle) and verifies Gerrit boots and the data is intact
    THERE — the live production volume is never detached, mutated, or replaced during a drill,
    so a failed drill cannot endanger production. Defense-in-depth: the live data volume
    carries `lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true }`. **Back-out:** a failed drill is unwound by
    terminating the throwaway instance/volume (the live system is untouched). For the genuine
    disaster-recovery case (where you DO replace the live volume), the runbook documents a
    back-out — detach-but-RETAIN the old volume, attempt the restore, and re-attach the
    retained original at the same device path if the restore fails — performed only after a
    plan review that removes `prevent_destroy`.
- **Monitoring:** CloudWatch basic + an alarm on "gate down" (Gerrit/MCP unreachable) so an
  outage — which freezes `main` — is noticed.
  - **Probe mechanism (resolves the no-ALB / plain-EC2 gap).** Because d251 excludes an ALB,
    CloudWatch has no native HTTP-reachability metric for the on-box services. The plan pins
    one concrete mechanism: a **cron/systemd-timer health probe on the EC2 host** runs every
    60 s, curls Gerrit (`curl -sf http://localhost:8080/config/server/version`) and the MCP
    health endpoint, and publishes a binary custom metric via
    `aws cloudwatch put-metric-data --namespace Rebar/Gate --metric-name GerritReachable
    --value 1|0`. The `aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm` in `monitoring.tf` fires when
    `GerritReachable < 1` for **2 consecutive 60 s periods**, and sets
    **`treat_missing_data = breaching`** so that if the on-box probe itself stops publishing
    (e.g. the host crashed and the timer is dead) the alarm transitions to **ALARM**, not the
    CloudWatch default `INSUFFICIENT_DATA` — a host crash that silences the probe must raise
    an alert, not go unnoticed. This detects process/port-level gate-down (a Gerrit/MCP crash
    the probe can still report). **Host-crash backstop:** because a hard host failure also
    kills the probe, a second `aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm` on the EC2
    `StatusCheckFailed_System` / `StatusCheckFailed_Instance` metric (AWS-published, independent
    of the on-box probe) backs the probe alarm so a dead host is detected even if the
    `treat_missing_data` path were missed. Both alarms point at the same SNS topic. The probe
    script and the metric namespace/name are the explicit interface contract documented in the
    monitoring runbook so a Gerrit upgrade that moves the health path is a known, versioned
    change point.
  - **Notification delivery (so an outage is actually noticed).** The alarm's `alarm_actions`
    point at an **SNS topic** whose subscription endpoint is the team alert target
    (email/Slack/PagerDuty). The endpoint is stored in **SSM Parameter Store** (consistent
    with d251's secrets-at-boot pattern) and read via `data.aws_ssm_parameter` in
    `monitoring.tf` — not committed as a plaintext `.tfvars` value. An alarm with empty
    `alarm_actions` does not satisfy this story.
  - **IAM scope.** Enumerate the minimum IAM actions the new resources need — CloudWatch
    (`PutMetricAlarm`/`PutMetricData`/`DescribeAlarms`), DLM (the snapshot lifecycle role with
    `ec2:CreateSnapshot`/`ec2:DescribeVolumes` scoped to the data volume), SNS
    (`CreateTopic`/`Publish`) — scoped to the specific resources (no `cloudwatch:*`/`dlm:*`
    wildcards), added to the existing instance profile or a dedicated scoped DLM role.
- **Terraform state.** `infra/terraform/monitoring.tf` uses the **shared remote backend**
  established by the earlier infra stories (S3 state + DynamoDB locking, `us-east-1`), not
  local state, so concurrent applies during a drill cannot corrupt state; the runbook makes
  `terraform plan` (reviewed) a required step before `apply`.
- **Runbooks:** provision/restore + the one-minor-at-a-time Gerrit upgrade.
- **Sequencing (de-risk order).** Execute E2E proof first (validates the live gate) ->
  monitoring alarm (observability of that gate) -> restore drill (recoverability) ->
  runbooks (document the above); each step is evidence-gated on the previous.

## Scope
In: the e2e proof, the restore drill, monitoring, runbooks. Out: the full four-pass
dogfood (b744-WS6); choosing the *final* alarm threshold/period tuning and the *final*
RTO target beyond the stated starting values (operational tuning, left to the implementer
within the mechanism/bounds fixed here).

## Files
- `infra/runbooks/*.md` — provision/restore runbook (includes RTO target, snapshot
  identification, `prevent_destroy` handling, `terraform plan`-before-`apply`) and the
  one-minor-at-a-time Gerrit upgrade runbook (documents the probe health-endpoint contract).
- `infra/terraform/monitoring.tf` — CloudWatch alarm (`GerritReachable < 1` for 2 periods)
  + SNS topic/subscription (endpoint from SSM) + DLM snapshot lifecycle policy
  (`retain_rule.count = 7`) + scoped IAM; uses the shared S3+DynamoDB backend; adds/asserts
  `lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true }` on the stateful data-volume resource.
- The host health-probe script (cron/systemd timer) publishing the `Rebar/Gate/GerritReachable`
  custom metric (co-located with the host provisioning assets).
- An e2e test script under `infra/tests/` (push->review->vote->submit->replicate assertion,
  the negative-path GitHub-direct-push-rejected assertion, the `LLM-Review`=MAX label-value
  assertion read against `project.config`, and the timeout/terminal-state fallback logic).

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] E2E proof passes: a test change is reviewed + voted + submitted in Gerrit and appears on
      GitHub `main`; the e2e test asserts the cast `LLM-Review` value equals the label range's
      MAX (as defined in d251 `project.config`); a direct GitHub push / PR-merge to `main` is
      rejected. The test bounds the vote wait with a timeout and reports "no vote / harness
      failure" distinctly from "gate correctly blocked."
- [ ] A restore-from-backup drill succeeds on a SEPARATE throwaway instance/volume restored
      from the latest snapshot (the live production volume is never detached/mutated during the
      drill), within the **pre-stated ≤ 2-hour RTO target**, with the achieved RTO recorded;
      the drill's restore source is an ON-DEMAND QUIESCED snapshot (Gerrit briefly stopped/
      read-only before the snapshot) and excludes `index/` and `cache/`; routine RPO snapshots
      are scheduled crash-consistent DLM snapshots (git-recoverable via `fsck`) — the two tiers
      are reconciled, not contradictory; merged history is confirmed intact on GitHub. The live
      data volume carries `lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true }`, and
      the restore runbook documents a back-out (terminate the throwaway on a failed drill; for
      real DR, detach-but-retain the original and re-attach on failure).
- [ ] A gate-down condition raises an alert: the host probe publishes the
      `Rebar/Gate/GerritReachable` custom metric, and the CloudWatch alarm fires
      (`GerritReachable < 1` for 2 consecutive periods) and delivers a notification via its
      SNS topic to the configured (SSM-sourced) endpoint — `alarm_actions` is non-empty and a
      simulated gate-down delivers an actual notification. The probe alarm sets
      `treat_missing_data = breaching`, and a simulated **host-down** (the probe stops
      publishing entirely, as in a host crash) also raises an alert — both via the probe
      alarm going to ALARM on missing data and via a dedicated EC2
      `StatusCheckFailed_System`/`StatusCheckFailed_Instance` alarm backstop — rather than
      sitting silently in `INSUFFICIENT_DATA`.
- [ ] A DLM/`$site_path` snapshot retention policy is in effect (DLM `retain_rule.count`
      bounded; default 7) so snapshots do not accumulate unbounded, and IAM for the new
      monitoring/DLM/SNS resources is scoped to specific resources (no wildcard actions).
- [ ] Provision/restore + Gerrit upgrade runbooks exist under `infra/runbooks/`, documenting
      the RTO target, snapshot identification, the probe health-endpoint contract, the
      `prevent_destroy` removal procedure, and `terraform plan`-before-`apply` on the shared
      remote backend.

## Round-7 remediation
- **SNS alert endpoint SSM path (E2):** the SNS subscription endpoint is stored in SSM at **`/rebar/prod/alert-endpoint`** (read via `data.aws_ssm_parameter` in `monitoring.tf`, not a plaintext tfvar).
- **review_code decision rule (G6):** `rebar.llm.review_code()` returns a `review_result` (a `findings` list), NOT a verdict string. The receiver derives a binary decision: **BLOCK if any finding is at/above a configured blocking severity (default critical/high), else PASS** -> PASS->label MAX, BLOCK or error/no-result->-1 (fail-closed); the threshold is config-driven. b744-WS6 swaps in `gate_dispatch.produce_code_review_verdict` (typed `{verdict: PASS|BLOCK}`), removing the derivation.
- [ ] `monitoring.tf` reads the SNS endpoint from SSM `/rebar/prod/alert-endpoint`, and the e2e asserts the receiver's derived PASS/BLOCK decision maps to the LLM-Review MAX/non-MAX value.

## Round-12 addition (manual re-review / `/rerun` runbook)
S4b adds a token-authenticated manual re-review endpoint on the review-bot receiver
(`POST /rerun?change=<id>`, reached via nginx at `/review/rerun`) that FORCE re-reviews a
change — bypassing the dedup + existing-vote short-circuits — so an operator can recover a
change stuck at a fail-closed `-1` (e.g. from a transient LLM outage) WITHOUT amending the
patchset. It is still fail-closed (a rerun only requests a FRESH review; it can never force a
PASS). This MUST be clearly documented in S7's runbooks so a rebar developer/operator can find
it and use it.

Added AC:
- [ ] The `infra/runbooks/` runbooks include a clear, discoverable "manually re-run a review"
      section documenting the `/rerun` endpoint: WHERE it lives (the review-bot receiver, via
      nginx `/review/rerun`), HOW to call it (the exact authenticated `curl` with the
      `?token=` secret sourced from SSM `/rebar/prod/gerrit-bot-token` and the `change` id),
      WHEN to use it (recover a change stuck at a fail-closed `-1`; vs. the automatic 5-minute
      backfill reconciler which only re-reviews vote-LESS changes), and that it is fail-closed
      (requests a fresh review, never forces a PASS). Cross-referenced from ADR-0009.
